stem: A telecommunications communication network uses 3 parallel relays for redundancy. The message delivered requires at least one relay to function. relay 1 has a reliability of 88%. relay 2 has a reliability between 70% and 84% (uncertain due to limited operational data). relay 3 has a reliability of 77%. Additionally, there is a common-cause failure probability (e.g., power outage, shared software bug) that could disable all relays simultaneously, estimated between 4.0% and 6.3%. What is the overall probability that the message delivered?

ground_truth.answer: P(message delivered) ranges from 0.9292 to 0.9558. Point estimate: 0.942.

solution_steps: P(system works) = 1 - P(all 3 relays fail simultaneously).
At lower bound: P(all fail) = 0.12 × 0.3 × 0.23 = 0.0083. P(works) = 0.9917.
At upper bound: P(all fail) = 0.12 × 0.16 × 0.23 = 0.0044. P(works) = 0.9956.
With common-cause failure (4.0%-6.3%): P(works) = (1-P_cc) × P(independent works). Range: [0.9292, 0.9558].
common_failure_modes: Multiplying individual reliabilities instead of computing 1 - P(all fail)
Using a single reliability for the uncertain component without propagating uncertainty
Treating the system as a series (all must work) rather than parallel (any one suffices)
Ignoring common-cause failures that can disable all paths simultaneously
